subject.title: Example: digital health passports
subject.question: How did digital health passports govern populations through risk, mobility, and proof of health status?
subject.executive_thesis: The health passport connected collective protection with differentiated control over mobility: it operationalized proof of health while normalizing classification of bodies by risk and compliance.

interests.name: Turn contagion into a verifiable mobility problem
interests.rationale: Movement became governable through certificates, risk thresholds, and proof of health status.

actors.name: Vaccinated, tested, and uncertified persons
actors.rationale: The population was segmented by proof status that conditioned access to selected spaces.

tools.name: QR certificate and access control
tools.rationale: The QR code converted medical status into an authorization signal readable by institutions, platforms, and public venues.

narrative.name: Health responsibility as condition of participation
narrative.rationale: The responsible subject is framed as someone who makes health status verifiable to protect others.

results.name: Differentiated access to social space
results.rationale: Access to work, transport, or leisure was modulated by health status, with both inclusionary and exclusionary effects.

feedback.rationale: Resistance, litigation, and changing epidemiological conditions forced adjustments to validity periods, exemptions, and control conditions.

items.actor: Bodies, attention, fear, desire, identity, health, and productivity
items.mechanism: Health status becomes a readable authorization signal attached to bodily risk and mobility.
items.incentive: Make individual bodies legible and governable in everyday movement.
items.norm: The responsible person proves health status before participating.
items.outcome: Bodies are sorted by risk and compliance status.
items.resistance: Refusal to disclose health status or challenge certificate mandates.
items.power_shift: Authority moves closer to bodily status and self-management.

items.actor: Families, schools, workplaces, media, and peer groups
items.mechanism: Social pressure frames verification as care, responsibility, and solidarity.
items.incentive: Stabilize compliance before direct coercion is needed.
items.norm: Unverified participation becomes socially suspicious or irresponsible.
items.outcome: Belonging and access become linked to visible compliance.
items.resistance: Counter-narratives defend privacy, bodily autonomy, or proportionality.
items.power_shift: Social norms become an enforcement layer.

items.actor: State, public health authorities, border agencies, police, and regulators
items.mechanism: Law, emergency rules, eligibility categories, and official certificates condition access.
items.incentive: Increase administrative legibility, compliance, and risk management.
items.norm: Mobility is legitimate when backed by state-recognized proof.
items.outcome: State capacity expands through population classification.
items.resistance: Court challenges and sunset clauses contest emergency powers.
items.power_shift: Administrative classification gains direct effects on everyday life.

items.actor: Employers, venues, travel operators, insurers, and service providers
items.mechanism: Access to work, transport, leisure, and services is priced or conditioned by proof status.
items.incentive: Keep markets open while distributing risk and liability.
items.norm: Economic participation requires demonstrable risk compliance.
items.outcome: Survival and participation are translated into credentialed behavior.
items.resistance: Informal workarounds, boycott behavior, or market exit.
items.power_shift: Market access becomes a behavioral discipline mechanism.

items.actor: Platforms, employers, app vendors, data processors, and compliance vendors
items.mechanism: Private infrastructures verify, store, display, and gate access through QR workflows.
items.incentive: Capture compliance infrastructure, operational data, and dependency.
items.norm: Private gatekeepers become normal administrators of public-risk rules.
items.resistance: Procurement scrutiny, privacy audits, and limits on data reuse.

items.actor: Borders, airlines, international institutions, and cross-border regimes
items.mechanism: Interoperable certificates sort mobility across jurisdictions.
items.incentive: Restore travel while controlling cross-border risk and political liability.
items.norm: International mobility depends on recognized health credentials.
items.outcome: Populations are stratified by document compatibility and vaccine recognition.
items.resistance: Diplomatic disputes over recognition, equity, and exclusion.
items.power_shift: Credential standards become geopolitical mobility infrastructure.

items.actor: QR systems, identity databases, mobile apps, biometrics, and verification APIs
items.mechanism: Technical mediation automates status checks through scanning, matching, and validation.
items.incentive: Scale governance while making decisions appear neutral and procedural.
items.norm: Automated verification becomes trusted evidence of eligibility.
items.resistance: Offline alternatives, minimal-data designs, and technical audits.
items.power_shift: Technical standards shape practical rights and access.

items.actor: UX flows, defaults, notifications, access friction, and status cues
items.mechanism: Behavior is shaped through convenience, friction, visible status, and default pathways.
items.incentive: Produce compliance before users experience the system as coercive.
items.norm: The easiest path is to verify, update, and present proof on demand.
items.outcome: Choice architecture redistributes autonomy toward system-approved behavior.
items.resistance: Manual alternatives, refusal, spoofing attempts, or civil disobedience.
items.power_shift: Interface design governs conduct at the point of decision.

actions: Condition ordinary access on health proof
Extend status checks beyond direct clinical care
items.interpretation: A protective logic also produced an infrastructure of sorting and conditional mobility.

items.name: Durable normalization of risk proof
early_signals: Extension into insurance or employment contexts
disproven_if: Certificates are sunset without institutional reuse
items.rationale: A proof infrastructure can outlive the crisis that justified it.

items.claim: Health certification linked medical status to social access.
items.source_note: Verify against the studied country or jurisdiction.
items.counter_evidence: In some contexts, certificates were time-limited and removed after the crisis.

items.assumption: Verification infrastructures can be reused for other risk regimes.
items.disproving_test: No observable institutional reuse or extension after the crisis.

quality_gate.next_improvement: Add jurisdiction-specific legal and empirical sources.
